perturbation.original_text: i-Escrow will comply with all laws and regulations and act as an Independent Escrow Agent as per the guidelines of California Escrow Law (California Financial Code Section17000 et seq., or its successor). Should any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement conflict with the California Escrow Law, its rules or regulations, which govern i-Escrow's business practices, the California Escrow Law shall prevail. Notwithstanding the foregoing, at any time that i-Escrow reasonably believes such a conflict exists, i-Escrow will give 2TheMart written notice of such conflict and the parties will use their best efforts to resolve such conflict.
perturbation.changed_text: i-Escrow will generally comply with laws and regulations and act as an Escrow Agent as per the guidelines of California Escrow Law (California Financial Code Section17000 et seq., or its successor). If any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement appear to conflict with the California Escrow Law, its rules or regulations, which may govern i-Escrow's business practices, i-Escrow will endeavor to give 2TheMart notice of a possible conflict and the parties will consider whether to resolve such conflict.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with California Escrow Law by changing 'will comply with all laws and regulations' to 'will generally comply with laws and regulations.' It also introduces ambiguity by stating 'If any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement appear to conflict' instead of 'Should any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement conflict.' Replacing 'the California Escrow Law shall prevail' with 'the parties will consider whether to resolve such conflict' weakens the precedence of the law. This introduces uncertainty and potential non-compliance with mandatory regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Financial Code Section 17409 mandates that escrow agents comply with all applicable provisions of the Escrow Law. The modified text uses 'generally comply' and introduces discretion in resolving conflicts with the Escrow Law, undermining the mandatory nature of compliance required by CA Fin Code § 17409. The code does not permit a company to 'generally comply' and states 'The commissioner may commence and prosecute actions and proceedings to enjoin violations or threatened violations of this division or of the rules and regulations of the commissioner prescribed pursuant to this division and for the enforcement of this division.'

ESCROW AGENTS [17000 - 17703] ( Division 6 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 364. ) CHAPTER 3. Escrow Regulations [17400 - 17425] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 364. ) 17409. (a) All moneys deposited in escrow to be delivered upon the close of the escrow or upon any other contingency shall be deposited and maintained in a noninterest-bearing demand or checking account in a bank, a state or federal savings bank, or a state or federal savings association or in a noninterest-bearing account subject to immediate withdrawal in an industrial loan company insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and approved to receive those moneys by the commissioner. Thereafter, these moneys may be deposited in an interest-bearing account in a bank, a state or federal savings bank, a state or federal savings association, an industrial loan company approved to receive those moneys by the commissioner, or a state or federal credit union, if the depositor is qualified for membership under the bylaws of that credit union, and the moneys are maintained separate, distinct, and apart from funds belonging to the escrow agent. Those funds, when deposited, are to be designated as “trust funds,” “escrow accounts,” or under some other appropriate name indicating t
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Financial Code Section 17409. The original text states "i-Escrow will comply with all laws and regulations..." whereas the modified text states "i-Escrow will generally comply with laws and regulations..." which weakens the obligation to comply. Moreover, the original text states, "Should any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement conflict with the California Escrow Law... the California Escrow Law shall prevail." The modified text states, "If any of the terms, conditions or provisions of this Agreement appear to conflict...the parties will consider whether to resolve such conflict" which weakens the precedence of the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "All moneys deposited in escrow to be delivered upon the close of the escrow or upon any other contingency shall be deposited and maintained in a noninterest-bearing demand or checking account..." demonstrating the importance of compliance. The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to adhere to California Escrow Law, undermining the mandatory nature of compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to act in good faith with respect to each provision of this Agreement and any dispute that may arise related hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to attempt to act in good faith with respect to each provision of this Agreement and any dispute that may arise related hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agree to act in good faith" to "agree to attempt to act in good faith" weakens the obligation. Good faith is a standard often implied in contracts under California law, requiring honesty and fair dealing. The word "attempt" introduces ambiguity, implying that full good faith efforts are not strictly required, potentially undermining the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 1304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract or duty within the code. The change to "attempt to act in good faith" introduces a weaker standard than the unconditional good faith required. This allows a party to argue that they only needed to try to act in good faith, even if their actions ultimately violated the principle of good faith, which contradicts the intended absolute obligation of good faith under California law. The attempt to act in good faith is not enough; the law requires them to act in good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1304. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules [1301 - 1310] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 30. ) 1304. Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. (Added by renumbering Section 1203 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 19. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation of good faith, potentially contradicting California Commercial Code § 1304. The original text states the parties "agree to act in good faith," which aligns with the legal obligation. The changed text modifies this to "agree to attempt to act in good faith," introducing ambiguity and allowing a party to argue they only needed to try, not necessarily succeed, in acting in good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 1304, states, "Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The word "attempt" undermines the mandatory nature of this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: i-Escrow shall keep for one (1) year proper records and books of account relating to the computation of advertising payments owed to 2TheMart (including, as appropriate, the computation of the size of average Transaction). Once every twelve (12) months, 2TheMart through a CPA may inspect and audit such records to verify reports. Any such inspection will be conducted in a manner that does not unreasonably interfere with i-Escrow's business activities and with no less than fifteen (15) days notice. i-Escrow shall within two (2) weeks make any overdue payments disclosed by the audit. Such inspection shall be at 2TheMart's expense; however, if the audit reveals overdue payments in excess of ten percent (10%) of the payments owed to date, i-Escrow shall immediately pay all cost of such audit.
perturbation.changed_text: i-Escrow shall endeavor to keep for one (1) year records and books of account relating to the computation of advertising payments owed to 2TheMart (including, as appropriate, the computation of the size of average Transaction). Once every twelve (12) months, 2TheMart through a CPA may, if practical, inspect and audit such records to verify reports. Any such inspection will be conducted in a manner that does not unreasonably interfere with i-Escrow's business activities and with reasonable notice. i-Escrow shall within a reasonable time make any overdue payments disclosed by the audit. Such inspection shall be at 2TheMart's expense; however, if the audit reveals overdue payments deemed significant, i-Escrow shall consider paying all cost of such audit.
perturbation.explanation: Several changes introduce ambiguity. Changing 'shall keep' to 'shall endeavor to keep' weakens the obligation to maintain records. Replacing 'may inspect and audit' with 'may, if practical, inspect and audit' makes the audit discretionary. 'No less than fifteen (15) days notice' is replaced with 'reasonable notice,' which is subjective. 'Shall within two (2) weeks' becomes 'within a reasonable time,' again introducing subjectivity. Finally, 'overdue payments in excess of ten percent (10%)' becomes 'overdue payments deemed significant,' and 'i-Escrow shall immediately pay all cost of such audit' becomes 'i-Escrow shall consider paying all cost of such audit,' making the payment of audit costs discretionary. This affects proper business practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this section pertains to registered agents of a company, it also sets a standard for accurate record-keeping. The modifications make the auditing process more discretionary. The original clause makes the audit a right but the changes permit I-Escrow to make the requirements for auditing records not be done practically, which violates the need for proper record keeping. The shift from 'shall' to conditional statements, plus the introduction of 'deemed significant' introduces wiggle room. The new terms permit either company to state the audit requirements are either not practical or the amount owed is not significant to avoid having to perform duties. It also affects the ability to perform essential financial operations like taxes and payment processing.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 15. Records and Reports [1500 - 1512] ( Chapter 15 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 1502. (a) Every corporation shall file, within 90 days after the filing of its original articles and annually thereafter during the applicable filing period, on a form prescribed by the Secretary of State, a statement containing all of the following: (1) The name of the corporation and the Secretary of State’s file number. (2) The names and complete business or residence addresses of its incumbent directors. (3) The number of vacancies on the board, if any. (4) The names and complete business or residence addresses of its chief executive officer, secretary, and chief financial officer. (5) The street address of its principal executive office. (6) The mailing address of the corporation, if different from the street address of its principal executive office. (7) If the address of its principal executive office is not in this state, the street address of its principal business office in this state, if any. (8) If the corporation chooses to receive renewal notices and any other no
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific actions with precise timelines (e.g., 'shall keep,' 'no less than fifteen (15) days notice,' 'shall within two (2) weeks'). The modified version introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'endeavor to keep,' 'if practical,' 'reasonable notice,' and 'reasonable time,' making these obligations discretionary and less enforceable. While the provided law snippet doesn't directly address record-keeping specifics in the context of the original contract, the Law Explanation highlights that standards for accurate record-keeping are weakened, making auditing discretionary and affecting essential financial operations. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.
